title: Cat Foul-Smelling Dark Ear Discharge: Triage and Care Guide
meta_desc: Learn what dark, smelly ear discharge in cats means, why it is a medium-priority concern, and how photos help your vet provide the right treatment.
content_html:
What Is Dark, Foul-Smelling Ear Discharge?

Noticing a dark, pungent discharge from your cat's ear can be concerning. This symptom is most commonly associated with ear mites, yeast infections, or bacterial overgrowth. The dark material is usually a combination of earwax, blood, and inflammatory debris produced by the body in response to irritation or infection. A strong, foul odor typically points toward an active infection that requires professional medical intervention.

Is It an Emergency?

The urgency level for this condition is Medium. While usually not a life-threatening emergency that requires an immediate ER visit tonight, it is a painful condition that should not wait more than 24 to 48 hours for an appointment. If your cat is also exhibiting a head tilt, loss of balance, or extreme lethargy, you should seek veterinary care sooner, as these can be signs that the infection has reached the inner ear.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Taking a clear, high-quality photo of the affected ear can be an invaluable tool for your veterinary team. A photo allows the triage professional to see the color and texture of the discharge—distinguishing between the 'coffee-ground' appearance of mites and the moist, yellow-brown discharge of yeast. It also helps them see the degree of swelling or redness in the ear canal, allowing them to better assess your cat's pain level and prioritize your appointment.

faqs.question: Can I use over-the-counter ear drops?
faqs.answer: It is not recommended to use over-the-counter drops without a diagnosis. If the eardrum is ruptured, certain medications can cause permanent deafness or neurological issues.

faqs.question: Should I clean the ear before the vet appointment?
faqs.answer: No, please avoid cleaning the ear before your visit. The veterinarian needs to take a sample of the discharge to look at under a microscope to identify exactly what is causing the infection.

faqs.question: Are ear infections painful for cats?
faqs.answer: Yes, ear infections are significantly painful. Cats are experts at hiding discomfort, but scratching at the ear or shaking the head are clear signs of distress.
